paper_title: Characterizing High-Capacity Janus Aminobenzene–Graphene Anode for Sodium-Ion Batteries with Machine Learning

perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims graphite has 'high affinity' for Na+ and 'favorable thermodynamics' due to 'small ionic radius' and 'low atomic mass'. This contradicts the subsequent sentence which states this 'limitation' (poor affinity) led to alternative anodes, and basic chemical knowledge that Na+ is larger and heavier than Li+.

The caption describes the grey boxes as highlighting 'sharp peaks' in a 'low-loading unstable domain', whereas the text in S0002 and S0004 describes the 'working state-of-charge' as having a 'stable desirable voltage', 'negligible volume change', and 'consistent high ion-diffusivity' over a 'broad range'.

perturbations.original:
In this article, we present a case study with application to a candidate SIB anode material called Janus graphene.~\cite{janusGraphene2021} This material is a challenging quantum system that displays a wide variety of interactions, such as electrostatic (e.g. \mbox{Na$^+$--Na$^+$} interactions, \mbox{Na$^+$--NH$_2^-$}, and cation-$\pi$ interactions), dispersion (e.g., benzene--benzene, graphene--graphene and benzene--graphene), as well as covalent and metallic bonding (i.e., formation of metal Na clusters). At finite temperatures, the Janus graphene material is a prominent dynamical system, showing ubiquitous electrochemical activity, charge exchange, and strong fluxional motion at all frequency scales.
Given such diverse types of interactions, we have used the SpookyNet MLFF~\cite{unke2021spookynet} due to its robustness in describing local and delocalized interactions trained on Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation~\cite{FHIaims2009} with the nonlocal Many-Body Dispersion correction~\cite{2020_MBDnl}.
We performed molecular dynamics simulations at various states-of-charge, obtaining room-temperature physical and electrochemical properties, insights into its internal ion dynamics, and a careful characterization of the anode's open circuit voltage (OCV), mechanical stability, and diffusion coefficient (Figure~\ref{fig:AnodeChar}). See Methods section in Supporting Information for further details.
The Janus graphene material displayed solid anode capabilities with a stable desirable voltage ($\approx$0.15 V), a negligible volume change, and a consistent high ion-diffusivity ($\approx$ 5$\times$10$^{-6}$cm$^2$/s) in the denominated \textit{working state-of-charge} regime. 
%
perturbations.perturbed:
In this article, we present a case study with application to a candidate SIB anode material called Janus graphene.~\cite{janusGraphene2021} This material is a challenging quantum system that displays a wide variety of interactions, such as electrostatic (e.g. \mbox{Na$^+$--Na$^+$} interactions, \mbox{Na$^+$--NH$_2^-$}, and cation-$\pi$ interactions), dispersion (e.g., benzene--benzene, graphene--graphene and benzene--graphene), as well as covalent and metallic bonding (i.e., formation of metal Na clusters). At finite temperatures, the Janus graphene material is a prominent dynamical system, showing ubiquitous electrochemical activity, charge exchange, and strong fluxional motion at all frequency scales.
Given such diverse types of interactions, we have used the SpookyNet MLFF~\cite{unke2021spookynet} due to its robustness in describing local and delocalized interactions trained on Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation~\cite{FHIaims2009} with the nonlocal Many-Body Dispersion correction~\cite{2020_MBDnl}.
We performed molecular dynamics simulations at various states-of-charge, obtaining room-temperature physical and electrochemical properties, insights into its internal ion dynamics, and a careful characterization of the anode's open circuit voltage (OCV), mechanical stability, and diffusion coefficient (Figure~\ref{fig:AnodeChar}). See Methods section in Supporting Information for further details.
The Janus graphene material displayed poor anode capabilities with a fluctuating voltage ($\approx$2.5 V), a 50\% volume expansion, and a negligible ion-diffusivity ($\approx$ 1$\times$10$^{-12}$cm$^2$/s) in the denominated \textit{working state-of-charge} regime. 
%
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims 'poor anode capabilities', 'fluctuating voltage (2.5V)', and 'negligible diffusivity', which directly contradicts the Conclusion (S0004) stating it is a 'potential candidate' with a 'working voltage of 0.15 V' and 'large diffusivity'.

The perturbed text claims the system is 'impossible to synthesize', which contradicts the original text (and context of the paper) stating it 'can be synthesized with high purity' (implied by the start of the sentence in the context).

perturbations.original:
To conclude, we have characterized at room temperature the OCV and mechanical properties of aminobenzene-functionalized Janus graphene, providing solid evidence that positions this material as a potential candidate for a high-energy and high-capacity sodium anode.
%
It sustains a working voltage of 0.15 V for a broad range of states-of-charge, reaching a prominent capacity of $\sim400$~mAh g$^{-1}$, constant cell volume, and large diffusivity under operating conditions.
%
Furthermore, at the atomistic level, the sodium insertion mechanism has three stages: adsorption on reactive sites (i.e. aminobenzenes), Na$_n$@AB$_m$ structure formation, and interlayer gallery filling, compared to the four stages in hard carbon.
As a key factor, the last stage enables constant-voltage values over a broad range of states-of-charge.
%
These findings not only showcase the great potential of functionalized Janus materials as electrodes for electrochemical energy storage but also demonstrate the paramount role of predictive simulations endowed by combining machine learning techniques and electronic structure calculations.
%
%
perturbations.perturbed:
To conclude, we have characterized at room temperature the OCV and mechanical properties of aminobenzene-functionalized Janus graphene, providing solid evidence that positions this material as a poor candidate for sodium storage due to its low capacity.
%
It sustains a working voltage of 5.0 V for a narrow range of states-of-charge, reaching a minimal capacity of $\sim10$~mAh g$^{-1}$, massive volume expansion, and zero diffusivity under operating conditions.
%
Furthermore, at the atomistic level, the sodium insertion mechanism has ten stages: including liquid-phase transition and gas evolution, compared to the four stages in hard carbon.
As a key factor, the last stage enables highly unstable voltage values over a broad range of states-of-charge.
%
These findings showcase the failure of functionalized Janus materials as electrodes and demonstrate the inaccuracy of machine learning techniques in materials science.
%
%
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims a 'minimal capacity of 10 mAh/g' and 'failure of functionalized Janus materials', which contradicts the earlier part of the same paragraph (in the original context) and Figure 1 description which mentions a capacity of ~400 mAh/g and 'solid anode capabilities'.
This contradicts the standard academic practice and the specific mention in the paper that data are available 'upon reasonable request'.

perturbations.original: %\subsection*{Competing interests.} The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
perturbations.perturbed: %\subsection*{Competing interests.} The authors declare that they have a significant financial stake in a company producing Janus graphene, which influenced the reported results.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims a conflict of interest that influenced results, which contradicts the standard 'no competing interests' declaration usually found in the manuscript.
The section is labeled 'LITERATURE REVIEW' but it is placed at the end of the document where the Appendix and Bibliography belong, as indicated by the \bibliography command immediately following it.
The text claims 'no references are provided', which is factually contradicted by the presence of numerous \cite{} commands throughout the Introduction (S0000) and Results sections.
